author: @Ludifant
text:
I have been looking into structured water and there seems to be some new research that contradicts the initial 2014 findings a bit.
The structure in water only seems to exist on the femto-second  (as shown with the X-rays) scale and averaged out on any other scale. So how there can be observable long term effects of any structure is not yet known. It is still theoretically possible, since we have dynamical systems theory to explain these types of behaviour as strange attractors. But they are very hard to study. It is interesting stuff.
I like that you approach it from the practical side, that may lead to things we can study and maybe give some more idea of what is actually going on.

author: @michaelransom820
text:
I'm having a hard time seeing why this isn't just a galvanic reaction?

In the presence of oxygen, metals oxidize. Other atmospheric effects and thermal cycling accelerates this process. When moisture is present, these oxidized salts form electrolytes between the two dissimilar metals and become a simple battery.

I know you started with deionized water, but the moment you put a metal or carbon ink into the water it will strip some of the metal and carbon ions and become an ionic solution again. 
This becomes especially true once you add methyl blue.

From this perspective, it is obviously a weak solution as the galvanic potential of Al and Cu is 0.53v, but your voltage would probably climb with time as EC increased. 

Furthermore adding a light source would do exactly what we see here as it would react with the metal surfaces via the photoelectric effect further increasing the EC of the solution. Is there something here i have missed??

I have just learned of the EZ water work of Dr. Gerald Pollack, and although i am skeptical i am also hopeful that there is in fact substance to his assertions. Although what he is claiming sounds at least plausible and i'm fairly certain there is at least a kernel of truth there, i have yet to find a satisfactory explanation of mechanism, especially considering the fact that certain aspects seem to directly contradict our (or at least my) current understanding of both chemistry and physics (ie; ionization of water via non-ionizing radiation).
Thanks a bunch.
Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i'm not sure what your question is - Pollack goes through the mechanism via his TED talk - there's nothing contradictory there - this is interesting not sensational

author: @l4m4re
text:
Hi Robert,

Yes, this is very exciting and interesting stuff. I have been analysing a/o Stanley Meyer´s "car on water" for quite some time and I believe this EZ layer gives an explanation for what is known mostly as "Brown's gas" or "HHO". 

First of all, Pollack talks about "hydrophilic" substance, which is a substance which attracks water, because it's molecules can be polarized. This is exactly what is called a "dielectric" in Electrical Engineering. When you look at how electrolytic capacitors are constructed, you will find that you have two plates, of which the positive plate is an actual capacitor plate, while the negative plate is merely a contact plate to make electrical contact with the electrolyte. The actual negative capacitor plate is formed by the ions in the electrolyte. 

Another very interesting device are old-school electrolytic rectifiers, which can be home-made with aluminum and baking soda, as can electrolytic capacitors. I believe the existence of an EZ layer just atop the dielectric layer explains why an electrolytic rectifier works. And it also shows that this EZ layer is not only influenced by light, but can also be manipulated with electric fields.

I am currently working on a project in order to attempt to make a replication of Stan Meyer's first prototype of his "car on water", which appears to have stopped working around 1985 or so. I wrote an article about that, in which I was convinced electropolished stainless steel would provide a suitable dielectric layer:

http://www.tuks.nl/wiki/index.php/Main/StanMeyersSecret

And based on that, I started my project:

http://www.tuks.nl/wiki/index.php/Main/MeyerWFCReplicationProject

However, it turned out that a standard electropolishing process does not result in anything resembling a reasonable electrolytic capacitor. Therefore, I am currently experimenting with nikkel plated copper tubes on a small scale, which I just started doing. The idea is to grow a layer of Ni(OH)_x crystallic structures on the nickel plate, which should result in a reasonable electrolytic capacitor, since nickel is also used in super capacitors.

Now I'm not a chemist and there are at least two variations of Ni(OH) molecules, and there are two distinct charge exchange mechanisms acting in super capacitors, one of which is "pseudo capative" and involves chemical reactions instead with actual charge transfer, which is not wanted for the application of manipulating the EZ layer with an electric field. 

However, what I want to try is to fill the concentric electrolytic capacitor construction with a 10% NaOH solution, as is being done in Edison's Nickel-Iron batteries to see what happens. Some playing around with that showed the positive tube growing a black layer, which does suggest some form of Nickel Hydroxide developing.

Now when we succeed in building a reasonable electrolytic capacitor consisting of concentric tubes, we have something with which we can manipulate the EZ layer on a macro scale, just as with any ordinary electrolytic capacitor. And if we bring the electrolyte in between the tubes into acoustic resonance, much like as with an organ pipe, I believe we can manipulate this EZ layer to such a degree that some kind of crystalic water-like gas is obtained, which is being reported as imploding instead of exploding and being negatively charged.

To finish this off, I also have a theory of where the energy comes from, based on the work of Prof. Turtur, as you can read in the articles posted above.

If you are interested, you can contact me via lamare at gmail dot com.

Regards,

-- Arend --

author: @MongrelShark
text:
The aliminum could be acting as an air battery. Especially with carbon and water in the equation. If you have time, try it with oxidised copper on one electrode to create a p-n junction, and ditch the Al.

Have you googled hydrophobic battery before? There are a number of versions, none of them reduce in charge, ever. Some have been running for 60+ years. Continuiosly suplying current. Its all in the piddly amounts of power ranges, but the batterys have hardly been played with either, The common one thats easy to reproduce involves alcohol based hand sanitizer gel floating on water. One electrode in each material. Gives a few hundred mv and maybe a micro amp. But the cell size has no relation to power... A drop of water beading on a hydrophobic leaf gives the same power... If you could do this on a micro or nano scale..... The greater the hydrophobic strength, the greter the eletrical charge. Lots of overhang on water beads may be a factor, more experimentation on tiny scales is needed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: intersting stuff mate - could be an air battery but i doubt it - still - it could be. I'll look into this more thanks for the direction

replies.text: The de ionised water makes air battery unlikely, I agree, but there is a remote chance...

author: @joeglory425
text:
Great idea, mate! Homemade graphene is certainly much cheaper than nafion! If you add pH indicator dye to the water do you get an acid/base color separation? I imagine you would but it would be nice to know for sure as further confirmation to see if it behaves like nafion. I find it all very fascinating. I doubt mainstream science has accepted Pollack's ideas despite the evidence he presents. Part of that may be because nafion is so expensive. Now maybe more people will start looking into this phenomenon and repeating Pollack's experiments. 

Do you have any recommendations for choosing appropriate size magnets to make a magnetic device for aligning graphene?
